Q1: I had a pretty consistent study routine, honestly. I'd dedicate about 25 hours a week to studying, usually spreading it across mornings before my other activities and some evenings. I liked having a schedule written out, even if I didn't always stick to it perfectly. I'd block out specific times for each of my four online courses and try to rotate through them systematically. Having structure just makes me feel more in control of everything.
Q2: I definitely preferred regular times each week because it helped me stay on top of things without feeling overwhelmed. That said, I'd adjust a bit when deadlines were approaching or if I had extracurricular commitments. But mostly, I tried to keep things predictable because that's just how I function best. Routine keeps my stress levels low, and I've learned that about myself over the years.
Q3: Usually I'd log into the LMS first thing when I sat down to study, almost like checking email. It became automatic for me. I'd check for any announcements, see if there were new discussion posts to respond to, or look at upcoming deadlines. Sometimes my peer study group would message about something, and that would prompt me to hop on too. It was just part of my daily academic rhythm.
Q4: Yes, definitely! After the first few weeks, logging in became as natural as checking my phone in the morning. I think it happened because I made it a habit to start every study session by opening the LMS. My peers were also pretty active, so seeing their posts and interactions made me want to stay connected. The peer support aspect really helped cement it as a routine rather than a chore.
Q5: The video lectures were incredibly useful to me—I watched over 130 minutes of content because I could pause and take detailed notes. The discussion forums were also great because I could read and write out my thoughts, which suits my learning style perfectly. I found that interacting with my peers there helped me understand concepts better than just reading alone. Written explanations and reading materials were my go-to resources.
Q6: I'll admit I didn't complete all the assignments—I think I only finished about 18% of them, which sounds bad but I was strategic about it. Some assignments felt repetitive or didn't seem to add much to my understanding, so I'd prioritize the ones worth more or that covered material I was struggling with. I also sometimes skipped interactive simulations because I learn better through reading and writing anyway.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly connected to exam content or helped clarify something confusing. I also valued activities that let me engage with my peers because those discussions often gave me new perspectives. If something felt like busywork or didn't have clear learning outcomes, I'd deprioritize it. I'm practical about my time since I'm juggling multiple courses and extracurriculars.
Q8: Optional activities definitely felt different—there was less pressure, which sometimes made them more enjoyable but also easier to skip. Required activities had deadlines attached, so they got done first no matter what. With optional stuff, I'd do it if I had extra time or if it genuinely interested me. I appreciated having the choice, but I won't pretend I did all of them.
Q9: Absolutely, I did a lot of studying offline that wouldn't show up in the LMS. I'd download PDFs and readings, then print them out or read them on my tablet without being connected. I have textbooks and external resources I'd cross-reference too. A lot of my actual learning happened with physical notes spread out on my desk, writing summaries by hand because that helps me retain information better.
Q10: There were many times I'd be deeply studying but the LMS wouldn't capture it at all. For example, I'd spend hours reviewing my handwritten notes or working through practice problems from a textbook. I also studied with my peer group in person sometimes, discussing concepts without being logged in anywhere. My read-write learning style means I process a lot through writing things out, which happens on paper, not online.
Q11: Yes, this was pretty common for me. I'd download a lecture PDF or reading once, then study it multiple times offline over several days. The LMS would only show that single download, but I might have reviewed that material five or six times. I preferred having materials saved locally so I could access them anywhere, even without internet, and annotate them in my own way.
Q12: There were a few weeks mid-semester when my extracurricular activities got really intense, and I found it harder to engage as deeply. But honestly, my stress stayed pretty low throughout because I had good support systems in place. My peer group was helpful—when I felt a bit disconnected, chatting with them brought me back. It never became a major issue for me.
Q13: Competing responsibilities definitely affected my LMS usage at times. When I had a lot going on with extracurriculars or other courses, I'd be more selective about what I engaged with online. I'd focus on essentials rather than exploring everything available. But I never felt truly overwhelmed because I managed my time fairly well. Fatigue was occasional but not a major barrier for me.
Q14: Honestly, yes—there were times I'd scroll through materials or have a video playing but my mind was elsewhere. I'd technically be logged in and accumulating watch time, but I wasn't really absorbing anything. When I noticed this happening, I'd usually stop and come back later when I could focus better. It felt pointless to just go through the motions.
Q15: I did postpone some assignments, especially ones that felt less important or that I thought I could catch up on later. Sometimes I'd avoid starting something because I wasn't sure how to approach it, even though I knew it mattered. My low anxiety meant I didn't panic about it, but I recognize that my assignment completion rate suffered because of this tendency. I prioritized exams over assignments.
Q16: Deadlines definitely shaped my LMS activity. I'd be more active in the days leading up to a due date, making sure I'd reviewed the relevant materials and participated in discussions. Without a deadline, I might let things slide a bit. The structure of having clear due dates helped me stay accountable, even if I didn't complete everything. I worked better with that external pressure.
Q17: Feedback was motivating when I received it. If I got comments on a discussion post or assignment, I'd be more likely to engage again and try to improve. Positive feedback especially made me feel like my participation mattered. When feedback was delayed or absent, I sometimes felt less connected to the course. The peer support intervention helped because I got informal feedback from classmates regularly.
Q18: The course structure gave me a framework to follow, which I appreciated. Having modules organized clearly made it easy to plan my study schedule. The regular deadlines kept me moving forward even when motivation dipped slightly. I think the structure suited my routine-oriented approach well. Without that organization, I probably would have struggled more to stay on track.
Q19: The discussion forums and peer interaction features made engagement easier for me because I thrive on written communication. Having materials available for download was also great for my offline study habits. What made it harder was when there were too many small tasks—it felt fragmented and I'd lose track. Clearer prioritization of what was essential versus optional would have helped.
Q20: I think students might be exploring, trying to understand the material, but struggling to actually produce work. They could be reading and watching everything but not feeling confident enough to submit assignments. Or maybe they're dealing with personal issues that make it hard to complete tasks even when they're trying to stay engaged. High activity doesn't always mean high output—sometimes it's about searching for understanding.
Q21: That actually resonates with me somewhat. My attendance and assignment completion rates were low, but I still passed with a decent exam score. I did a lot of learning offline through my own notes and resources. The LMS activity doesn't capture everything—someone can be genuinely engaged with the material without constantly being logged in. Efficiency matters more than raw screen time.
Q22: Course logs can accurately show patterns like login frequency, time spent on videos, and participation in discussions. But they miss the quality of engagement—whether someone is actually thinking deeply or just clicking through. They also miss offline studying, peer conversations outside the platform, and the mental effort involved. Logs capture behavior, not learning.
Q23: Being engaged means I'm actively thinking about the material, connecting it to what I already know, and trying to apply it. It's not just about showing up or logging in—it's about being mentally present. For me, engagement includes discussing ideas with peers, writing out my understanding, and genuinely caring about learning the content. It's an internal state more than an external behavior.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I learned through handwritten notes, printed readings, and in-person study sessions with my peer group. It wouldn't capture the conversations that helped me understand difficult concepts or the time I spent thinking through problems away from my computer. My engagement was real and consistent, even when it didn't leave a digital trace. The data only tells part of the story.
